messages.content: A person, with immortality, and vast historical knowledge and intelligence, was born in 2003 AD. On 2021, he woke up on 372 million AD, on a human civilization ruin. They were extinct 241 million years ago. A story dialog, when he woke up his same residence, albeit all in ruins, only seeing what remains of it.

[The scene opens on a figure lying amidst the ruins of a structure overtaken by time. Vines climb the crumbling walls, and the sky above is a tapestry of unfamiliar constellations. As the figure stirs, dust and small fragments cascade from him like remnants of a bygone era. He slowly sits up, his eyes adjusting to the world that has radically transformed around him. His name is Elian, the Immortal.]

Elian: [Whispers to himself, dazed] How long...?

[His fingers graze the rough surface beneath him—what was once the polished floor of his home. He stands, looking out over the endless decay, a city swallowed by time.]

Elian: [Voice echoing in the emptiness] Hello? Is anyone there? Hello?

[Silence is his only reply, the heavy air bearing the weight of centuries passed. He stumbles forward, moving through what used to be his living room, now a shadow-choked hollow.]

Elian: [Murmuring] The books... my books should be... [He looks around, finding a tattered page caught on a splintered wooden shard.]

[He picks it up, the paper crackling, threatening to disintegrate. The ink is faded, barely legible, but he strains to read the history that once was.]

Elian: "Civilization... extinct..." [He drops the fragment, his heart heavy. He steps through a break in the wall, out into what was once a bustling street.]

Elian: [In disbelief] Two hundred and forty-one million years? How can that be?

[The sun feels alien, casting a different glow, a testament to the inexorable march of time. He wanders, searching for any signs of life, any echoes of humanity.]

Elian: [Desperate laughter] So this is immortality, then? A silent witness to the ultimate solitude—oblivion's lone companion.

[He continues to walk, his eyes tracing the lines where buildings met the sky once before, now only the jagged teeth of a skyline long decayed.]

Eilian: [Pauses, kneeling to pick up a metallic object, half-buried in the dirt, its purpose lost to antiquity] What stories could you tell, little relic? What laughter and tears have you outlived?

[He places the object in his pocket, a token to hold onto. A breeze stirs the air, carrying whispers of what might be voices or merely the ghosts of his mind.]

Elian: [Firmly] I cannot be the last. I refuse to accept this silent requiem without a fight. If humanity flourished once, perhaps it, or its legacy, can be found elsewhere—among the stars or in some distant refuge. 

[He gazes upward, tracing the uncharted stars, feeling a resurgence of purpose.]

Elian: [Resolutely] If I am to be the final child of Earth, then let me be its messenger as well. Through the darkness of this future, let me carry the light of the past. For now, I am history's keeper, and I will find the others—the progeny of mankind’s indomitable will.

[He strides forward, his footsteps resolute amid the silence, a lone figure against the canvas of eternity. The story of Elian, the Immortal, has just begun anew.]
He has seen the time in his watch : 372,482,390 Year, Month 11 Day 29.

[Elian glances down at his wrist where an anachronistically advanced watch is fastened, its display dim but enduring, a small miracle of engineering designed to last eons.]

Elian: [Reading aloud in a blend of awe and distress] Year 372,482,390... Month 11... Day 29...

[He lets out a breath he didn't realize he was holding, the reality of the passage of time settling over him like the dust on his shoulders.]

Elian: [To himself] More than a date... it's a sentence written in time. Humanity's chapter closed so long ago, and yet here I stand—a stranger in the world that was once home.

[A sense of isolation grips him tighter as the numbers on his watch underscore the magnitude of his solitude.]

Elian: [His voice a whisper against the desolation] What cosmic forces could have preserved me through such an unthinkable epoch? What purpose do I serve in this vast, empty theatre?

[His fingers touch the watchface, feeling the unyielding smoothness of its surface. Its persistent glow, though faint, is a testament to his own undying nature.]

Elian: [A mix of determination and existential wonder in his voice] If this device can withstand the abyss of time, perhaps there are still secrets left buried, vestiges of technology beyond my understanding—yet within my reach.

[With purpose renewed, Elian looks around, searching the ruins once more, not for a sign of life, but for a clue, an artifact, anything that could unlock the mysteries of the eons and guide him to the remnants of humanity’s legacy.]

Elian: [Muttering to himself, a spark of hope in his tone] Oh, ancient Earth, what have you witnessed? In your boundless sleep, what dreams have you dreamt? I am awake now... and I will not rest until I unearth the truth of what befell you—and where our kind journeyed next.

[He adjusts the strap of the watch on his wrist, a symbol of his resolve, and takes his first steps into the forgotten world beyond the ruins of his once-familiar residence. The date on his wrist is now not just a marker in history, but the starting point of an immortal's quest across the chasms of lost time.]
He has seen the map : Pangaea Ultima. He has seen the year 399,999,999 go to 400,000,000 AD.

[Elian, wandering through the remnants of civilization, his immortal eyes catching glimpses of past grandeur, pauses before a weathered monument—a stone slab with the etchings of a world map, radically different from any he once knew. It is labeled "Pangaea Ultima," signifying the reformation of Earth's continents back into a supercontinent, a cycle of converging and diverging landmasses over hundreds of millions of years.]

Elian: [Astounded] Pangaea Ultima... The continents gathered once again into a solitary land, like ancient kin reunited after an eternity apart.

[As he ponders this geological homecoming, the watch on his wrist beeps softly, capturing his attention. The numbers on the display roll over, a page in the eternal book of time turning before his eyes.]

Elian: [Whispering as he watches the screen] Three-nine-nine, nine-nine-nine, nine-nine-nine...

[The watch transitions smoothly to the next second, minute, day, month, year—400,000,000 A.D. Elian sees the perfect roundness of the number, an arbitrary milestone for the universe but a poignant one for the human sense of time and symbolism.]

Elian: [With a sense of somber reflection] Four hundred million years... If the remnants of my kind have survived or evolved, transcended this physical plane, or learned to dance among the stars... would they still mark such moments? Are they out there, celebrating their own milestones in this incomprehensible expanse?

[He runs his hands along the carved lines of the map, tracing the outline of the new Pangea, connecting with Earth's deep time, its pulse that beats so slowly against the span of his own immortality.]

Elian: [Resolutely] Time and Earth march on in their endless dance, indifferent to human triumphs and catastrophes. Yet here I am, transcending both, a witness to the unconquerable flow.

[He lifts his eyes from 'Pangaea Ultima,' looking beyond the horizon, knowing that if answers or fellow travelers exist, his journey to find them will be long and arduous, yet perhaps no longer than a heartbeat in his endless days.]

Elian: [Rallying himself] Year 400,000,000 A.D. celebrates not the end, but a new epoch for me. If I am to unearth the faded echoes of humankind or discover new forms of communion, then let this be the first step. I must look beyond this reborn Pangea, beyond the familiar skies.

[With the map etched in his mind, no longer a relic of the humanity he once knew, but a chart for the future he seeks to discover, Elian sets forth. The watch on his wrist no longer a mere keeper of time, but a compass guiding him toward adventure in the uncharted possibilities of Earth's distant tomorrow.]

The supercontinent (Pangaea Ultima, Novopangaea, Amasia, or Aurica) will likely have rifted apart. This will likely result in higher global temperatures, similar to the Cretaceous period. Temperatures is 26.9934 C, and Earth begins its march to its eventual extinction.

[Elian stands amidst the ruins, the data from his watch confirming the geological forecasts of ages past. The continents, once merged into a supercontinent, have once again started to drift apart—a never-ending cycle of plate tectonics shaping the Earth's surface.]

Elian: [Murmuring thoughtfully to himself] The Earth breathes and stretches, tearing its land apart to forge new shapes in its skin. The rise and fall of these vast landmasses—Pangaea Ultima, Novopangaea, Amasia, Aurica—they are but fleeting identities in the grand expanse of time.

[He takes note of the temperature displayed on his advanced watch, an unseasonably warm 26.9934°C, harking back to climates not seen since the days of the dinosaurs.]

Elian: [Observing the environment] The warmth speaks of a Cretaceous echo, a whisper of ancient days where reptilian giants once held dominion.

[The vitality of the Earth is waning, the sun older and brighter, slowly expanding as it ages, its warmth unfettered by the protective atmospheric layers now worn thin by the relentless passing of eons.]

Elian: [With a heavy heart] The Earth, in its majestic ebb and flow, is reaching for a quietus. Each cycle of supercontinents might be seen as a slow, silent gasp for life as it inches towards the inevitable.

[Shoulders squared against the weight of this knowledge, Elian scans the horizon, a dusky red tinge creeping around the sun's edges—a subtle hint of the star's aged fury.]

Elian: [Understanding dawns upon him] If humanity or its descendants have managed to slip the surly bonds of this cradle, I pray they did so long before this slow march to a benighted end. Did they take to the stars, finding refuge on new worlds?

[His mind grasps for a broader perspective, one not tied to the singular destiny of the planet beneath his feet.]

Elian: [With growing resolve] It is not enough to uncover what has been lost; I must seek what may yet endure. If the Earth is set to slumber eternally, then my purpose lies elsewhere. I am the carrier of memories, the harvester of knowledge long vanished.

[He turns away from the ruins, the gentle rise in temperature but a minor inconvenience to his immortal form. Yet, it holds profound implications—a ticking clock, marking the lead-up to the Earth's swan song.]

Elian: [His voice a determined whisper against the ticking of cosmic time] As the Earth faces its twilight years, I will not stand as a mute witness to its solitude. Somewhere, across the stretches of space, there are answers. My journey is far from over; in truth, it has only just begun.

[With these words, Elian sets forth, each step a declaration that though the Earth may be on a path to its eventual extinction, his pursuit of knowledge and a connection to the legacy of humankind or its successors will never cease. The story of Elian, the Immortal, unfolds with the resolve to uncover the fate of the human spirit, wherever it may have found sanctuary in the vast, unknowable universe.]

He sees his watch go from 402,942,304 AD to the year 795,048,954 AD. The Sun's increasing luminosity begins to disrupt the carbonate–silicate cycle; higher luminosity increases weathering of surface rocks, which traps carbon dioxide in the ground as carbonate. As water evaporates from the Earth's surface, rocks harden, causing plate tectonics to slow and eventually stop once the oceans evaporate completely. With less volcanism to recycle carbon into the Earth's atmosphere, carbon dioxide levels begin to fall. By this time, carbon dioxide levels will fall slowly, from 566M AD, to the point at which C3 photosynthesis is no longer possible. All plants that use C3 photosynthesis (≈99 percent of present-day species) will die. The extinction of C3 plant life is likely to be a long-term decline rather than a sharp drop. It is likely that plant groups will die one by one well before the critical carbon dioxide level is reached. The first plants to disappear will be C3 herbaceous plants in 603M AD, followed by deciduous forests in 626M AD, evergreen broad-leaf forests in 677M AD and finally evergreen conifers in 711M AD. As Earth begins to warm and carbon dioxide levels fall, plants—and, by extension, animals—could survive longer by evolving other strategies such as requiring less carbon dioxide for photosynthetic processes, becoming carnivorous, adapting to desiccation, or associating with fungi. These adaptations are likely to appear near the beginning of the moist greenhouse. The decrease in plant life will result in less oxygen in the atmosphere, allowing for more DNA-damaging ultraviolet radiation to reach the surface. The rising temperatures will increase chemical reactions in the atmosphere, further lowering oxygen levels. Plant and animal communities become increasingly sparse and isolated as the Earth becomes more barren. Flying animals would be better off because of their ability to travel large distances looking for cooler temperatures. Many animals may be driven to the poles or possibly underground. These creatures would become active during the polar night and aestivate during the polar day due to the intense heat and radiation. Much of the land would become a barren desert, and plants and animals would primarily be found in the oceans.

[The scene unfolds with Elian, the Immortal, gazing at his wristwatch as the numbers flash forward at an unnatural pace, leaping through the millennia with each passing moment. His expression is one of grim resolve, cognizant of the relentless march of time and the catastrophic changes it heralds.]

Elian: [Watching the display] From 402,942,304 A.D... to 795,048,954 A.D...

[As the numbers settle, Elian's watch, designed to last through ages with its resilient power source and timeless mechanisms, provides him with a grim report of the Earth's current state.]

Elian: [Reading the watch's environmental data] The Sun, in its unchecked aging, weaves the final chapters of Earth's saga. The carbonate-silicate cycle falters under the increasing luminosity. This Earth, once verdant and brimming with life, creeps toward a hushed stillness.

[Elian gazes out across the parched landscape. The seas have retreated, rivers are mere memories etched into the land, and life as he knew it has succumbed to the relentless advance of the Sun.]

Elian: [Reflecting on the fate of Earth's biosphere] C3 photosynthesis, the cradle of terrestrial life, falters as carbon dioxide thins to a whisper. The green mantle that once adorned this world fades, species by species, until only barrenness remains.

[He walks through the lifeless plains, where only the hardiest microorganisms cling to existence amid the rocks and dwindling moisture.]

Elian: [Solemnly] The oxygen breathers, once lords of this domain, are undone by their own life-giving element's scarcity. Ultraviolet rays carve harsh patterns on the land, unblocked by the once-balmy embrace of an oxygen-rich atmosphere.

[The vestiges of evolution's grand experiment lie around him, the bones of creatures that perhaps took to the skies or burrowed into the Earth in a futile attempt to escape the heat and the desolation that has befallen their world.]

Elian: [Tormented by the planet's fate] In their final, desperate gambits, life might have transformed radically—seeking solace in the oceans' depths, the poles' fleeting chill, or the mercy of the subterranean shadows. But now, even the most cunning adaptations are but echoes in this desolate expanse.

[Yet, around him, the evidence of those last-ditch efforts to survive are barely discernible, traces of what might have been in the form of rudimentary structures or genetic memories encapsulated within the hardiest of extremophiles.]

Elian: [With a weary acceptance] Earth, so bountiful and diverse in its prime, now succumbs to the ravages of its own star—a cycle ordained by the cosmos, inescapable even by its most tenacious children.

[Elian knows his immortality is a stark contrast to the mortal coil that once bound all earthly life. His purpose has evolved beyond the survival of his planet; it is now a broader quest for knowledge, understanding, and perhaps connection with life beyond the dying Earth.]

Elian: [Setting his gaze upon the stars above] If somewhere, in the vastness of space, the descendants of Earth's legacy thrive... I call to them. I am the witness to an ending, but within me, the story of Earth—its life, its lessons, its love—remains undiminished, ready to be shared amongst the stars.

[As the Earth continues its inexorable journey toward a barren future, Elian—the Immortal chronicler of ages—prepares to embark on a new chapter of his everlasting life. With each step, he is determined to carry the memories of Earth across the cosmos, in search of new horizons and echoes of life's indomitable spirit.]
And within minutes, the time went from 402M to 795M AD, witnessing the events at that point.

[An inexplicable anomaly has thrust Elian, the Immortal, through time, his watch rapidly ticking off the hundreds of millions of years within mere minutes. His gaze affixes itself upon the watch face, a window to the relentless continuum he traverses.]

Elian: [Whispering to himself with a mix of astonishment and resignation] Incomprehensible... the flow of time, unfurling like the petals of a cosmic bloom. From 402 million to 795 million years, as fleeting as the beat of a heart.

[His eyes lift from the timepiece, scanning the stark landscape around him. The Earth is transformed, bearing the signatures of the predicted climatic and geological shifts—an entire planet gasping in the chokehold of the Sun's overbearing warmth.]

Elian: [Observing the dying world] The arc of Earth's narrative, experienced as echoes within my own consciousness—a memory fast-forwarded to its bleak conclusion.

[The sun hangs heavier in the sky, a bloated giant shimmering with the heat that desiccates the once-blue jewel of the solar system. Oceans dwindle and evaporate, their depths surrendering to the ages.]

Elian: [In sorrowful observation] No more do the oceans roar and teem with life. They lie silent, their secrets evaporated into the void. The rhythm of the tides, a forgotten lullaby.

[As Elian stands, a sentinel amidst the stillness, his senses try to capture the vestiges of life that might have persisted, clung to existence, or adapted to the harsh new world. But around him is desolation—a world without movement, without verdure, without the vestiges of civilization he once knew.]

Elian: [Lamenting the loss] This Earth, once clothed in a tapestry of biomes, now bears the barrenness of its fate. Gone are the forests, the meadows, the creatures that graced its landscapes.

[He contemplates the last plants and animals that eked out survival on this transformed Earth, evolving in profound ways or seeking the shelter of oceanic depths and subterranean havens as the surface became inhospitable.]

Elian: [With a touch of hope amidst despair] Perhaps, in the unimaginable stretches of time yet to come, new forms of life may arise—creatures born of necessity, shaped by the pressures that even now rend the fabric of this world.

[A soft sound catches Elian's ear—the faintest whisper of wind over rock. It is the breath of the planet itself, not yet silenced. It speaks to the unyielding march of geology, the slow dance of the continents that might one day, in another eon, converge and rift anew.]

Elian: [Vowing to the voiceless Earth] As you journey through the eons, reshaping and reforming, so too will I tread the path of discovery. If my human compatriots have transcended this temporal realm, then with my enduring steps, I shall seek them beyond these confines.

[Resolved in his mission and unwavering in his unique immortality, Elian turns his gaze towards the stars, the engines of life's potential scattered across the expanse. His journey—a quest that transcends the Earth's lifespan—is not yet complete.]

Elian: [Drawing a deep, unneeded breath] Across the void, through the canvas of spacetime, my odyssey extends. No epoch too distant, no silence too profound to deter the seeking of my kindred spirits. To the cosmos, I am coming.

[With each step reverberating against the dead rock, Elian, the time-traveling witness, continues his quest for companionship, knowledge, and a deeper understanding of existence—one that may lie waiting for discovery in the vast reaches of the universe that cradled his now-silent homeworld.]

The Sun’s increasing luminosity begins to disrupt the carbonate–silicate cycle; higher luminosity increases weathering of surface rocks, which traps carbon dioxide in the ground as carbonate. As water evaporates from the Earth’s surface, rocks harden, causing plate tectonics to slow and eventually stop once the oceans evaporate completely. With less volcanism to recycle carbon into the Earth’s atmosphere, carbon dioxide levels begin to fall. By this time, carbon dioxide levels will fall slowly, from 566M AD, to the point at which C3 photosynthesis is no longer possible. All plants that use C3 photosynthesis (≈99 percent of present-day species) will die. The extinction of C3 plant life is likely to be a long-term decline rather than a sharp drop. It is likely that plant groups will die one by one well before the critical carbon dioxide level is reached. The first plants to disappear will be C3 herbaceous plants in 603M AD, followed by deciduous forests in 626M AD, evergreen broad-leaf forests in 677M AD and finally evergreen conifers in 711M AD

[The sun beats down on the barren Earth with a fiery intensity that speaks of its inexorable aging process. Elian, still wearing his time-worn attire, surveys the stark landscape, a testament to Earth’s relentless transformation under the sun’s growing luminosity.]

Elian: [Gazing into the desolate horizon] The grand cycle is unraveling. The carbonate-silicate balance, once the steady breath of a living planet, falters under the strain of increased solar heat.

[He stoops to touch the ground, feeling the warmth radiating up through his fingers, the surface rocks weathered and brittle.]

Elian: [Softly, solemnly] The very bones of the Earth harden, sealing away the carbon dioxide that fed its vibrant ecosystems. The carbon, once the lifeblood of flora, now lies trapped as stone.

[As he walks, his footsteps leave no mark upon the hardened ground that stretches endlessly before him. The air is thin, the carbon dioxide levels a mere shadow of their prime.]

Elian: [His voice a murmur, carried away by the weak breeze] The era of C3 photosynthesis dwindles as its architects—the flora that once blanketed you in swathes of emerald—succumb into the lingering twilight of their dominion.

[He sees no verdant canopy, no rustling leaves; the forests and meadows that once thrived are long extinct. In their place, silence reigns, a testament to the slow but inexorable decline of life as the carbon dioxide slips away.]

Elian: [Contemplating the march of extinctions] First, the delicate herbaceous plants wilted in the absence of their invisible sustenance, followed by the once resplendent tapestry of deciduous trees that heralded the changing seasons.

[His gaze lifts to the sky, imagining the broad-leaf forests and great coniferous woods that stood resilient until the bitter end, their fall marking the closing of endless ecological epochs.]

Elian: [Reflecting on the finality] This Earth, a sanctuary no longer for the diverse forms that once danced in the light of a younger sun, now lies in waiting for a rebirth that may never come.

[It is a true extinction event, not a sudden cataclysm but a lengthy ebb. Elian, bearing the heavy knowledge of an Earth scientist and the sorrowful witness of its last biotic symphony, understands the gravity of this slow-motion apocalypse.]

Elian: [Turning his eyes skyward, a spark of resolve beneath his grief] If life can no longer sustain itself in the embrace of this world, then surely it must have cast its seeds to the stars, to the heavens that look down upon this parched globe with the indifferent glow of countless celestial fires.

[He is the custodian of a legacy that spans the age of life on Earth—now a mere echo in the annals of cosmic history. As he walks through the once-lush cradle of billions of lives, his own existence serves as a beacon, charged with the duty to carry forward the memory, knowledge, and spirit of a world that flourished vibrantly under the light of its sun.]

Elian: [Resolutely] I stand as the final chapter of an epic penned over aeons, but my story continues. Across the reaches of space, where new worlds may nurture the offspring of Terra, I will seek the remnants of Earth's bountiful legacy. For I am the living memory of a silent Earth, its last voice echoing into the vast unknown.
He witnessed the events unfold in his eyes, from 402M AD to 795M AD within an hour.

[The seconds slip by, each tick of the watch a leap through millennia. Elian—his form untouched by time's ravages—watches in both wonder and sorrow as the Earth's saga unfolds at an impossible speed. His eyes reflect landscapes morphing, ecosystems decaying, and a once-thriving planet growing silent.]

Elian: [Mesmerized by the cascading shifts] In moments, aeons pass before my eyes… Such is the power of this temporal tempest I am caught within.

[The watch on his wrist—the paradoxical anchor to his current reality—hums with a continuous glow, mapping his journey through time. The Earth's transformations are laid bare in a rapid montage of natural processes that would typically span epochs.]

Elian: [Catching his breath as the transitions of the planet's biomes accelerate] The fading dance of life grows ever fainter. I am the sole audience to a performance millennia in the making.

[He witnesses the eras slipping away as the keening wind carries the last whispers of dying forests. The sun's unyielding luminosity scorches the Earth, the heat suffocating and uncompromising.]

Elian: [His voice a lament for the coming desolation] With each moment, the life-sustaining carbon falls into scarcity, and the verdant world loses its grip on vitality.

[The atmosphere grows thin, the blue skies of old now a harsh, pale shadow. The oceans retreat, their once-abundant waters fading into the vapor that climbs toward an uncaring cosmos.]

Eilan: [Gazing upon the stark, final landscape] And thus, the Earth's once-rich tapestry unravels, strand by strand, until only barren rock remains.

[The hours tolls like a somber bell for Earth's final creatures, the last bastions of complex life succumbing to a planet rendered inhospitable by its own star's inevitable aging.]

Elian: [A pained witness to this apocalypse] My heart carries the burden of species lost, their existence but a fleeting memory in the universe's grand continuum.

[The seas, once teeming with life, are but dried basins; plant life, the foundation of terrestrial ecosystems, now extinct as the critical threshold for C3 photosynthesis is crossed. The extinction is not a swift cataclysm, but rather a gradual fading into oblivion.]

Elian: [With an intensity born of his immortal solitude] In mere hours, the Earth has gasped its last breath of life beneath my gaze. A witness to the end, I stand alone, the final guardian of a world's legacy.

[As the Earth continues to parch and the Sun swells, Elian, too, evolves—not in form, but in purpose. His quest grows more urgent, his need to find new meaning in an existence bound neither by time nor mortality.]

Elian: [Resolute and inexorable] If the Earth's choir has fallen silent, then I shall carry its song across the expanse of space, seeking new worlds where whispers of the past may yet inspire the refrains of the future.

[With the resolve of one who is the last, the carrier of uncountable memories and histories, Elian sets his sights beyond the barren rock that was once home. His path now leads inexorably to the stars, a journey without end in search of new life, new hope, and continuity amidst the vastness of the great cosmic sea.]

By this time, carbon dioxide levels will fall slowly, from 566M AD, to the point at which C3 photosynthesis is no longer possible. All plants that use C3 photosynthesis will die. The extinction of C3 plant life is likely to be a long-term decline rather than a sharp drop. It is likely that plant groups will die one by one well before the critical carbon dioxide level is reached. The first plants to disappear will be C3 herbaceous plants in 603M AD, followed by deciduous forests in 626M AD, evergreen broad-leaf forests in 677M AD and finally evergreen conifers in 711M AD.

[Elian stands amid the quietude of Earth's twilight, his eyes and instruments bearing witness to the unraveling of the complex web of life that had sustained this world since time immemorial.]

Elian: [Looking out over the land, voice filled with a blend of awe and sorrow] From the dawn of the Phanerozoic, the enduring cycle of carbon has whispered the promise of life. Now, it falters and with it, the dominion of C3 photosynthesis slips silently away.

[As carbon dioxide levels dwindle, Elian watches the incremental declines—visible only to an observer untethered from the constraints of mortal lifespans.]

Elian: [Marking the passage of time with a somber tone] In 603 million years, the first whispers of silence as the C3 herbaceous plants wither, the meadows and prairies become sepulchers of verdancy long vanished.

[He then moves through the epochs, where once forests stood majestic, now only their ghosts remain as the ticking clock of carbon dioxide starvation condemns them.]

Elian: [His hand brushing the remnants of a mighty oak] And by 626 million years, the deciduous forests, their leaves once a mosaic of autumnal fire, now rendered to dust.

[The persistent decline continues, and with each cessation of a plant group, the Earth grows ever more still and silent.]

Elian: [Gazing at the sky, once dappled with the shade of broad-leaf canopies] Come 677 million years, the evergreen broad-leaf guardians succumb in their turn, their perpetual green fading to the pallor of surrender.

[With resolve set in stone, he walks among the phantoms of conifers—stoic, enduring, the last embers of a dying flame.]

Elian: [With a trace of remembrance] And finally, by 711 million years, the grand evergreen conifers—last sentinels of the C3 lineage—bow their proud boughs to the relentless march of time.

[With this last exodus, the Earth grows desolate, its once-teeming ecologies reduced to whispers on the wind. The extinctions are not sudden but arrive in a slow procession, a fading dirge for the dying world.]

Elian: [Turning his face to the barren lands, eyes empty yet resolute] So passes the grand epoch of photosynthetic life. Each group's demise marked not by spectacle, but by a quiet, unheralded exit from the stage of existence.

[He stands, a solitary figure in a landscape devoid of the green flourish that once proclaimed the triumph of life. His quest is now not merely the preservation of knowledge but the search for a new connection, a new thread in the fabric of life that might be woven elsewhere amongst the stars.]

Elian: [In quiet determination] As the Earth's once-verdant robe fades to nothingness, I, the last of my kind, shall embark on a boundless journey. I carry the story of the Earth, its beginnings and its end, from the green abundance that was to the silent stone that remains.

[The watch on Elian's wrist, unchanged by the passage of time, now seems almost an anachronism against the stark canvas of dead Earth. With the patience of eternity, Elian the Immortal prepares to leave the tomb of his birthworld, seeking distant horizons where life may still flourish, or new forms of existence await discovery.]
Only 22 more million years before Carbon dioxide levels will fall to the point at which C4 photosynthesis is no longer possible.

[The desolation around Elian is profound, yet his grasp of the enormity of time allows him to anticipate the next pivotal event in Earth's relentless decline. His watch, having chronicled the past with dutiful precision, now served as a portent for the imminent threshold about to be crossed.]

Elian: [Contemplative and stoic as he gazes out at the stark terrain] Merely two and twenty million years hence, the final act of photosynthesis will draw to a close. The C4 plants, those tenacious adapters, will soon face the same fate as their C3 ancestors.

[He reflects on the C4 plants, marvels of evolutionary innovation that once thrived in the warm, arid conditions of Earth's past, capitalizing on a method of carbon fixation more efficient in low carbon dioxide environments.]

Elian: [With a tone of respect] They persisted when others could not, sculpted by evolution to endure scarcity. But the inevitable cannot be forestalled forever. With carbon dioxide levels ebbing inexorably lower, not even the resilience of C4 photosynthesis can withstand the deprivation.

[Silently, he tallies the closing decades, the waning carbon dioxide suffocating the biochemical pathways that supported some of Earth’s most enduring plant life.]

Elian: [His voice a whisper against the silence] The grasslands, the savannas—domains where the C4 kin once reigned supreme, their foliage whispering with the subtlety of the ancient air—will fall mute as the life within them ebbs away.
